lede: Chronicle (Tamnan) · ตำนาน › Tamnan accounts for 328 catalogued manuscripts. It clusters in Chiang Mai (46% of the corpus for this sub-genre), ahead of Phrae and Lampang. Nearly all (100%) are written in Tham Lanna · อักษรธรรมล้านนา script. By support it leans to palm-leaf · ใบลาน (100%) over mulberry paper (saa) · กระดาษสา (0%). Dated witnesses run 1760–1981 CE (231 of 328 carry a date).

findings.title: The heavyweights: the longest manuscripts
findings.gloss: The twelve longest texts by leaf count, led by one running to 1,046 leaves.

authored.title: Chronicle (Tamnan) › Tamnan
authored.body_html:
What this is

This is the tamnan proper — the foundation-legend in its purest form: the sacred history of a relic, a stūpa or a city, the story a place tells about how it came to be holy. At 328 witnesses it sits between the larger Buddhist Chronicle and the smaller Secular History, and its content is overwhelmingly the braḥ dhātu (relic) and mīöṅ (city) legends: tamnan of the relic at Lamphun, of Chiang Saen, of Haripuñjaya, of the founding of Chiang Mai.

A cataloguing signature worth noticing

The most striking feature of this sub-genre is not its content but its uniformity as a batch, which points to a single documentary source rather than a natural cluster. Three signals line up too neatly to be coincidence:

  • Language: 327 of 328 carry the identical tag Pali-Northern Thai — a label that appears almost nowhere else in the collection, where the norm is Pali and Lan Na.
  • Romanization: the titles are transcribed in a scholarly IAST-style system (tāṃnān braḥ dhātu càm dàṅ, tāṃnān yonaka jīeṅ sèn) quite unlike the phonetic Tanakhan / Wetsantala transliteration used across the rest of the catalogue.
  • Provenance: the holdings lead at Chiang Mai (152) and name distinctive repositories — Wat Pa Sak Noi, Wat Walukaram, and notably the Siam Society (18) — institutions tied to scholarly collection rather than temple survival.

Together these mark the Tamnan sub-genre as very likely a discrete digitized corpus folded into the catalogue — a different project's cataloguing conventions carried in with its records. That is a provenance fact, not a content fact, and it matters: counts and comparisons across this sub-genre should be read as "one source's holdings," not as an independent regional sample.

The shape of the collection

Almost entirely palm-leaf (327 of 328) and Tham Lanna, dated across 1760–1981. Beyond the Chiang Mai concentration it reaches Phrae, Lampang, Lamphun and — unusually — Bangkok (21), consistent with a scholarly collection that gathered witnesses centrally.

Notes

  • Verify the source in the raw metadata. Before treating any figure here as a regional signal, check `raw_metadata` / `source_identifier`: the uniform language tag and IAST titles strongly suggest these 328 share one origin catalogue, and the honest reading is a sourced batch, not a survival sample.
  • The relic-tamnan overlaps its sibling. In content this material is close to the Buddhist Chronicle's relic-histories; the split between them here is as much a cataloguing artifact as a substantive one, and merging or re-sorting the two is a live question.
  • Documentary, not efficacious. None are research-priority. The value is the dense, place-anchored corpus of foundation legends — and, as a bonus, a clean worked example of how provenance signatures hide inside an aggregated catalogue.
